titleStmt.title: Crack propagation at the interface between viscoelastic and elastic materials

funder.orgName: Italian Ministry of Education, University and Research (MIUR)

analytic.title: Crack propagation at the interface between viscoelastic and elastic materials

application.desc: GROBID - A machine learning software for extracting information from scholarly documents

div.p: Crack propagation in viscoelastic materials has been understood with the use of Barenblatt cohesive models by many authors since the 1970's. In polymers and metal creep, it is customary to assume that the relaxed modulus is zero, so that we have typically a crack speed which depends on some power of the stress intensity factor. Generally, when there is a finite relaxed modulus, it has been shown that the toughness increases between a value at very low speeds at a threshold toughness G 0 , to a very fast fracture value at G ∞ , and that the enhancement factor in infinite systems (where the classical singular fracture mechanics field dominates) simply corresponds to the ratio of instantaneous to relaxed elastic moduli.

Here, we apply a cohesive model for the case of a bimaterial interface between an elastic and a viscoelastic material, assuming the crack remains at the interface, and neglect the details of bimaterial singularity. For the case of a Maxwell material at low speeds the crack propagates with a speed which depends only on viscosity, and the fourth power of the stress intensity factor, and not on the elastic moduli of either material. For the Schapery type of power law material with no relaxation modulus, there are more general results. For arbitrary viscoelastic materials with nonzero relaxed modulus, we show that the maximum toughness enhancement will be reduced with respect to that of a classical viscoelastic crack in homogeneous material.
Viscoelasticity, crack propagation, cohesive models, bimaterial interfaces

div.head: Introduction
div.p: The problem of viscoelastic crack growth is of fundamental importance see the recent review of Rodriguez et al. (2020). Early investigations (Gent and Schultz, 1972, Barquins and Maugis 1981, Gent, 1996, Gent & Petrich 1969, Andrews & Kinloch, 1974, Barber et al, 1989, Greenwood & Johnson, 1981, Maugis & Barquins, 1980) noticed a steady state subcritical crack propagation with an enhanced work of adhesion G with respect to the adiabatic value G 0 namely

where V is velocity of peeling of the contact/crack line, a characteristic velocity was defined as V 0 = (ka n T ) -1 and k, n are (supposedly) constants of the material, with 0 < n < 1 where a T is the WLF factor to translate results at various temperatures T (Williams, Landel & Ferry, 1955).

From a more fundamental perspective, initially a paradox was identified by Graham (1969). Namely, since the stress field singularity is the classical inverse square root of elastic materials, at the crack tip we have infinite frequency and therefore an "elastic" material, which does not explain dissipation and speed dependence of toughness enhancement (Rice, 1978). But the paradox was solved by various authors (Schapery, 1975, Greenwood & Johnson, 1981, Barber et al., 1989, Greenwood, 2004 and others, see a review by Bradley et al.1997) using cohesive Barenblatt or Dugdale models removing the singularity in a cohesive zone whose size increases with speed (because of the toughness enhancement). Another school explains enhancement with estimating the dissipation (de Gennes, 1996), by the "viscoelastic trumpet" crack model, as the crack shape is different in the inner "glassy region", the intermediate "liquid region", and finally in the outer soft "rubbery" region. On this second school, notable improvements have been made by Persson & Brener (2005) who clarified the relevant range of frequencies involved in the estimating dissipation, and gave a solution for a general viscoelastic material.
Both schools suggest results qualitatively of the form (1) and introduce the cohesive strength of the material σ c and therefore introduce the length scale
div.formula: a 0 = G 0 E 0 2πσ 2 c (2)
div.p: where E 0 is the relaxed modulus (the modulus at zero frequency) and σ c is the cohesive stress. Also, all these models suggest looking at the remote stress intensity factor K as applied in remote regions as giving an effective toughness
div.formula: K 2 Ic (V ) = G (V ) E 0
div.p: , where E 0 is expected in remote regions, and hence obtain the maximum toughness enhancement as

For many polymeric or rubbery materials this ratio is very large possibly an increase of 3 or 4 orders of magnitude. Here, G (∞) stands for G (V → ∞) and E ∞ is instantaneous modulus or the modulus observed at infinite frequency of oscillatory loading. Remark however that in much of the literature on metal creep or polymers like that reviewed in Bradley et al.(1997), it is often assumed that the rheology corresponds to a zero relaxed modulus E 0 = 0 and hence there is no lower threshold for crack propagation. These studies obtain a power law for the crack propagation speed, of the type
div.formula: V ∝ K m I (4)
div.p: where m depends on details of the rheology. Notice that the Gent-Schultz kind of result Eq. ( 1) is fully compatible with this scaling, when one considers G >> G 0 so that (1) can be written as V ∝ K 2/n I i.e. m = 2/n. For a standard material it can be shown that in the intermediate range of velocities n = 1/2 and hence V ∝ K 4 I .

Therefore, as clearly explained by Wang et al ( 2016), the literature on ceramics or metal creep and on non cross-linked polymers see viscoelasticity as an "apparent weakening", since they compare to the elastic fast fracture limit, while the polymers literature shows viscoelastic results as a mechanism for enhanced dissipation "an apparent toughening" with respect to the threshold for the start of subcritical crack growth.

However, analysis of the case of a bimaterial crack between an elastic material and a viscoelastic one has not been attempted before to the best of our knowledge, at least not from a simplified perspective as we shall provide here. This is surprising, since the bimaterial crack problem has received much attention for the elastic-elastic case (there are at least two papers with more than 1000 citations, for example, Suo &Hutchinson, 1990, andComninou, 1977), while in many cases interface cracks occur between a rubbery or polymeric material peeling from an elastic surface (see Kendall's classical paper, Kendall (1975) again with nearly 1000 citations). This topic is now an emerging area of technology in adhesives and in Nature-inspired attachment systems, and in many cases the polymer will exhibit viscoelasticity. Therefore an analysis of the the problem is timely. Indeed, we show here a simple generalization of the cohesive model treatment for a bimaterial interface with viscoelasticity, obtaining some (approximate) closed form results.

div.head: A bimaterial crack propagation
div.p: If we consider a bimaterial interface with a semi-infinite crack, see Fig. 1, where one material is elastic and the other viscoelastic, we can assume a cohesive model and write the Energy Release Rate (ERR) as
div.formula: G = G e + G v = G 0 (5)
div.p: where G e is the ERR in the elastic material, and G v the ERR in the viscoelastic material. We consider the classical Schapery-Greenwood Dugdale cohesive model, and to address a semi-infinite crack, so we assume the conditions for K-field dominance, or Small Scale Cohesion (SSC) to be satisfied. In this case we have simply
div.formula: G e = 1 2 K 2 I E ; G v = 1 2 K 2 I C ef f b V (6)
div.p: where we consider plane stress for simplicity. For the viscoelastic material, we have introduced an effective compliance C ef f given by (Rice, 1978)
div.formula: C ef f b V = 1 0 C b V - b V λ df dλ dλ(7)
div.p: where C (t) is the viscoelastic compliance, relating the instantaneous strain
div.formula: ε (t) = t -∞ C (t -τ ) dσ(τ )
div.p: dt dτ to the history of stress σ (t) in uniaxial conditions. The function f (λ) is the opening (stretch) in the cohesive zone from the Dugdale model, given approximately in a bimaterial by

and the cohesive stress σ c cancels the singularity (in the Dugdale form where the cohesive strength is constant and uniform in the cohesive region). Under SSC (small scale cohesion), the length of this cohesive zone is approximately b = π 8
div.formula: K I σ c 2 (9)
div.p: In the appendices we justify the use of the Mode I stress intensity factor instead of the complex stress intensity factor in the rigorous treatment of bimaterial interfaces, and hence the approximations introduced in the last 3 equations.

div.head: A Maxwell material
div.p: We are not interested in giving a full solution for a general viscoelastic material, as we shall discuss qualitative features later. A convenient case is however that of a Maxwell material which has no relaxed modulus. In this case
where µ is viscosity. A full simple analytical solution is possible then by performing the integration (7)
div.formula: C ef f b V = 1 E ∞ + b 3µV (11)
div.p: Hence, substituting in the energy balance equation ( 5) (6, 11) we get1 G = 1 2
div.formula: K 2 I E + 1 2 K 2 I C ef f b V = 1 2 K 2 I 1 E + 1 E ∞ + b 3µV = G 0 (12)
div.p: and using ( 9) this reduces to
div.formula: 1 2 K 2 I 1 E * + π 24 K I σ c 2 1 µV = G 0 (13
div.p: where we define an combined modulus 1
This ( 13) leads then to the simple solution µV = π 24
Obviously, the critical condition of fast fracture is at
div.formula: V → ∞ when (15) gives K 2 Ic,∞ = 2G 0 E * ∞ (16)
div.p: Vice versa, for very low K 2 I << 2G 0 E * , we can write from ( 15)
div.formula: µV low = π 24 K 4 I 2G 0 σ 2 c (17
div.p: a simple scaling which does not depend on E * , and so on neither of the elastic moduli of the elastic and viscoelastic materials (but just on the viscosity). This is a typical results, when the viscous fracture-length scale is small and the stress field has the classical K-field dominance, see eqt.25 of Wang et al (2016), where the scaling is common in small-scale damage-zone models of creep-rupture in linear materials like the model of Cocks and Ashby (1982).

div.head: The Schapery power law forms of creep-compliance
div.p: More general exponents in the power law could be found if one uses a more general rheology for the material (but still assuming E 0 = 0) i.e. with more than one relaxation times or a continuous spectrum of relaxation times, like in much of the literature on metal creep or polymers as that reviewed by Bradley et al.(1997). Indeed, Schapery (1975aSchapery ( ,1975b) ) shows that one can use
div.formula: C ef f (t) = 1 E ∞ + C 1 d b V n (18
div.p: where d ≃ 1/3 is a corrective factor which depends very weakly on n ∈ [0, 1] (in our previous Maxwell model we showed it should be equal exactly to 1/3), and C 1 is a generalized viscosity with dimensions [ s -n M P a ]. For our bimaterial interface, repeating the analysis we obtain G = 1 2
div.formula: K 2 I E + 1 2 K 2 I C ef f b V = 1 2 K 2 I 1 E + 1 E ∞ + C 1 d b V n = G 0 (19) or 1 2 K 2 I 1 E * ∞ + C 1 πd 8 n K I σ c 2n 1 V n = G 0 (20) leading to V = πd 8 (C 1 E * ∞ ) 1/n (2E * ∞ G 0 -K 2 I ) 1/n K 2+2/n I σ 2 c (21
div.p: which at low load leads to a scaling with
div.formula: K 2+2/n I V low = πd 8 (C 1 ) 1/n (2G 0 ) 1/n K 2+2/n I σ 2 c (22
div.p: The last two equations generalize Schapery's result to a crack on a bimaterial interface using his assumed generalized rheology. Notice that in the power law regime the role of elastic modulus disappears again for this class of materials. Notice also that fast fracture occurs at the same level of stress intensity factor, independently of n, as we expect. We can rewrite (21) by taking the dimensionless factor
div.formula: K 2 = K 2 I 2E * ∞ G 0 , and defining a * ∞ = G 0 E * ∞ 2πσ 2 c V = V (C 1 E * ∞ ) 1/n a * ∞ = π 2 6 K 2+2/n 1 -K 2 1/n (23)
div.p: Fig. 2 shows some example plots of equation ( 23). 23) for n = 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1 (black, blue, red, green solid lines) in a viscoelastic/elastic bimaterial interface for a semi-infinite crack, as a function of dimensionless stress intensity factor

div.head: The case of non-zero relaxed modulus
div.p: Our Maxwell material analytical result is clearly instructive, and the generalization to the Schapery creep compliance form has proved quite useful in the engineering literature, see Bradley et al.(1997), as applied to quite general form of polymers or metal creep (Cocks and Ashby, 1982). It is hard to give exact simple results for the most general rheology and is outside of the scope of the present investigation. One point of interest for any viscoelastic material with finite relaxed modulus E 0 > 0, is that obviously C ef f (V → 0) = 1 E 0 , so that we would have the trivial energy balance equation
and so we now define another combined modulus 1
div.formula: E * 0 = 1 E + 1 E 0 to obtain K 2 Ic,0 = 2G 0 E * 0 (25
div.p: Comparing this with ( 16), we obtain the amplification
div.formula: A max K 2 Ic,0 = E * ∞ E * 0 = E ∞ E 0 E 0 E + 1 E∞ E + 1 (26)
div.p: and since we expect that for polymers or rubbery materials E∞ E 0 >> 1 (which is why we get the toughness amplification)
div.formula: A max = K 2 Ic,∞ K 2 Ic,0 < E ∞ E 0 (27
div.p: Thus the limiting toughness enhancement is less than that for the crack with the viscoelastic material on both faces. Fig. 3 shows the maximum amplification A max from (26) (black thick solid line) as a function of the ratio E/E 0 , as compared with an even simpler approximation, A max = E E 0 , which shows the maximum amplification is of the same order of the ratio E/E 0 in an intermediate range.

div.head: Conclusions
div.p: We have obtained a simple closed form solution for the subcritical propagation of a crack at the interface between an elastic and a terial, in the of Maxwell material, or for the more creep compliance rheology of Schapery. We find that the "elastic" fracture occurs for K 2

Ic,∞ = 2G 0 E * ∞ where the "equivalent" modulus is the inverse of the sum of the compliances of the elastic material and the (instantaneous value) of the viscoelastic material. The subcritical crack propagation for the Maxwell material depends only on the viscosity of the material, and not on any of the elastic moduli, and scales at low speeds with the fourth order of the stress intensity factor. For the Schapery creep compliance form, the results are quite similar, except there is a more general power law dependence. We also argue that for any more general viscoelastic material constitutive equation, having a relaxed modulus E 0 > 0, the toughness amplification would be less than that expected for the viscoelastic semi-infinite crack in a homogeneous material.
Wang, H., Lu, W., Barber

div.head: Appendix
div.p: The complex stress intensity factor due to a set of point forces applied to the surface of the crack at x 1 = x1 is (Rice and Sih, 1965)
div.formula: K = 2 π coshπε Q + iR (l -x1 ) 1 2 +iε(28)
div.p: where the x 1 -axis is the bimaterial interface, the crack occupies x 1 ≤ l, where l = b will be the cohesive zone size, and ε is the usual bimaterial index

where β is Dundurs' bimaterial constant, i = √ -1, R represents a pair of horizontal, equal and opposite point forces and Q pair of equal and opposite vertical forces. These forces are defined per unit thickness. The stress ahead of the crack on the x 1 -axis is (Rice, 1988)

The stresses in the cohesive zone are uniform and such that σ 22 + iσ 12 = σ c + iσ s , where σ c is the cohesive stress and σ s is a shear stress arising as a reaction to constraints on shearing motion relative to the interface. The net stress intensity factor is zero, so
div.formula: 2 π coshπε (σ c + iσ s ) b 0 dx 1 (b -x1 ) 1 2 +iε = K A (31)
div.p: where the cohesive zone occupies 0 ≤ x 1 ≤ b, and K A is the far-field applied stress intensity factor. This integrates to give π
div.formula: coshπε (σ c + iσ s ) √ bb -iε = (1 -2iε) K A (32)
div.p: We represent the cohesive zone σ c + = Σe (33)
the applied stress intensity factor as
div.formula: K A = K A K A e iψ (34
div.p: and note that b -iε = e -iεlnb (35)
div.formula: and 1 -2iε = 1 + 4ε 2 1 2 e -iarctan2ε(36)
div.p: As a result, we find that
div.formula: ϕ = ψ + εlnb -arctan2ε (37) b = π (1 + 4ε 2 ) K A A 8cosh 2 πε T 2(38)
div.p: and
div.formula: T 2 = σ 2 c cos 2 (ψ + εlnb -arctan2ε )(39)
div.p: Note that Eq. (37,39) must be solved simultaneously, probably by iteration. However, for possible material combinations that exclude auxetics ε ≪ 1 (Rice, 1988). In that case, a good, 1 st order estimate of b is the result for homogeneous systems
div.formula: b = πK 2 A 8σ 2 c (40
div.p: where K A is the mode I stress intensity factor in the far-field and we have assumed pure Mode I far-field loading. Now, return to the point force solution Eq.( 28) and add a second (auxiliary) set of point forces at x 1 = x1 (notice that usually x1 = x1 ) so that the stress intensity factor is
div.formula: K = 2 π coshπε F 2 + iF 1 (l -x1 ) 1 2 +iε + P 2 + iP 1 (l -x1 ) 1 2 +iε (41)
div.p: The complex conjugate of K is
div.formula: K = 2 π coshπε F 2 -iF 1 (l -x1 ) 1 2 -iε + P 2 -iP 1 (l -x1 ) 1 2 -iε(42)
div.p: The resulting energy release rate is (Rice, 1988)
div.formula: G (F 1 , F 2 , P 1 , P 2 , l) = c c 2 16cosh KK(43)
div.p: where, in plane strain,
and

with E i being Young's moduli and ν i Poisson The subscript 1 indicates the material in x 2 ≥ 0 and the 2 indicates the material in x 2 ≤ 0. Note that both sides of Eq. ( 43) are real. Let the displacements at x 1 = x1 on the top surface of the crack be c 1 (δ 1 , δ 2 ) /2, both components defined to be real. The displacements in the bottom surface of the crack are c 2 (δ 1 , δ 2 ) /2. Note that
and
Furthermore, using the following generalized Castigliano's theorem
and

where Ψ is the total potential energy, sum of the strain energy and the potential energy of the applied loads. Notice that (48,49) reduce to the classical Castigliano's theorem for a linear system, in which Ψ = -U where U is strain energy. As a result, as noted by Burns et al. (1978) we have Maxwell relationships
and
Hence, with P 1 = P 2 = 0, these lead to ∂δ 1 )
where
div.formula: ζ = ln l -x1 l - ε(54)
div.p: We convert Eq. ( 53) to the result for the cohesive zone with uniform stress. This leads to ∂δ 2 (x 1 )
div.formula: ∂l + i ∂δ 1 (x 1 ) ∂l = - (c 1 + c 2 ) (σ c + iσ s ) 4π(l -x1 ) 1 2 l 0 (cosζ -isinζ ) dx 1 (l -x1 ) 1 2 (55) With ε small, cosζ ≃ 1- ζ 2 2 = 1 -ε 2 ln l -x1 l -x1(56)
div.p: and sinζ ≃ εln l-x 1 l-x 1 . Using this, we deduce that the numerator in the integrand will only have a small imaginary part and the real part will be close to unity, namely
div.formula: l 0 (cosζ) dx 1 (l -x1 ) 1 2 ≃ l 0 dx 1 (l -x1 ) 1 2 -ε 2 l 0 ln l-x 1 l-x 1 dx 1 (l -x1 ) 1 2
div.p: Hence,
With ε small, we retain only the first term not depending on ε 2 , and integrate to obtain
Now introduce x = b -x1 as the magnitude of the distance from the tip of the cohesive zone. The result above then becomes
div.formula: δ 2 (x) ≃ - (c 1 + c 2 ) σ c 2π b x b + 1 2 1 - x b ln 1 + x b 1 -x b (59)
div.p: Based on the same deductions, the applied stress intensity factor causes a cohesive zone stretch given approximately by
div.formula: δ A (x) = (c 1 2 ) K 8π = (c 1 + c ) σ c b π x b(60)
div.p: where eqt.39 has been used to eliminate K A in favour of b. The total cohesive zone stretch is given by Eq. ( 59) added to Eq. ( 60), leading to
div.formula: δ 2,tot (x) ≃ (c 1 + c 2 ) σ c 2π b x b - 1 2 1 - x b ln 1 + x b 1 -x b (61
div.p: as assumed for our calculations (8). In retaining the second order term,
div.formula: δ 2nd 2,tot (x) ≃ (c 1 + c 2 ) σ c 2π b    x b - 1 2 1 - x b ln 1 + x b 1 -x b - ε 2 b b x1 2 l 1 2 -2 + ln l l-x 1 l -x1 1 2 d l  
div.p: (62) Note that c 1 gives the contribution due to the upper material and c 2 that of the lower material.

Fig. 4 shows the cohesive zone stretch due to the cohesive zone tractions only without the cohesive zone stretch due to the applied stress intensity factor i.e. from Eq.( 57) made dimensionless as δ 2 (x) ≃ δ 2 (x) / -(c 1 +c 2 )σc 2π b for ε = 0, (black thick solid line), and for ε = 0.1, 0.2, 0.3 to second order with blue, red and green lines. While the slope of the black line should be vertical at x/b the effect is very confined so that it is not apparent on the scale of Fig. 4. Notice that it can be shown by some lengthly algebra the stretch of the crack outside the cohesive zone can be found by considering absolute values of the quantities in the log terms, so we are plotting this result too in Fig. 4. 57) for ε = 0, (black thick solid line), and for ε = 0.1, 0.2, 0.3 to second order with blue, red and green lines.

div.head: Appendix 2 -Derivation of cohesive zone stretch result with viscoelasticity
div.p: The viscoelastic constitutive law is given by
div.formula: ε ij (t) = t -∞ C ijkl (t -τ ) dσ kl (τ ) dt dτ(63)
div.p: where ε ij is the strain, t is time, C ijkl (t) is the viscoelastic compliance and σ kl is the stress. Within a homogeneous material, Eq. ( 63) may be rewritten as
div.formula: ε ij (t) = t -∞ C ijkl (t -τ ) C -1 klmn (0) dε el mn (τ ) dt dτ(64)
div.p: where C -1 klmn (0) is the tensor of instantaneous elastic moduli and ε el mn (τ ) is the elastic strain at the current stress. We can integrate this with respect to position in the homogeneous material to obtain
div.formula: u i (t) = t -∞ C (t -τ ) C (0) du el i (τ ) dt dτ(65)
div.p: where C (tτ ) is an appropriate measure of the viscoelastic compliance and C (0) is the equivalent elastic compliance.

Following Rice's (1978) treatment of the homogeneous we apply Eq. ( 65) to of a crack along a bimaterial interface. We consider incompressible materials, so that, after approximation of the cohesive zone stretch, the contribution to the elastic stretch due to material 1 which is in the upper half of the plane, in a small cohesive zone on a semi-infinite crack, is
div.formula: δ el 1 = σ c b πG 1 x b - 1 2 1 - x b ln 1 + x b 1 -x b = σ c b πG 1 f (λ) (66)
div.p: where G 1 is the shear modulus of material 1, λ = x/b, with an equivalent result for δ 2 , the contribution from material 2 in the lower half plane. In Eq.66, x is measured from the tip of the cohesive zone where δ el 1 = 0 and x = b at the tip of the crack where the cohesive zone will break. Thus, the length of the cohesive zone is b and we have, as an approximation, b = π 8
div.formula: K A σ c 2 (67)
div.p: where K A is the applied Mode I stress intensity factor, used as an approximation of the complex stress intensity factor for a bimaterial crack, and σ c is the cohesive stress.
We define the viscoelastic compliance by
div.formula: γ (t) = t -∞ C (t -τ ) dσ s (τ ) dt dτ (68
div.p: where γ is shear strain and σ s is shear stress. Therefore, C 1 (0) = 1/G 1 and thus the viscoelastic contribution of material 1 to the cohesive zone stretch is
div.formula: δ 1 (t) = G 1 t -∞ C 1 (t -τ ) dδ el 1 (τ ) dt dτ(69)
div.p: Now consider a crack growing on the bimaterial interface, taken to be the x 1 -axis. The crack is growing at a rate V such that its tip, where the cohesive zone breaks, is at x 1 = V t and the tip of the cohesive zone is at
div.formula: x 1 = V t + b. As a result, x = V t + b -x 1(70)
div.p: We consider steady state growth, with K A and b both constant, so that
div.formula: dδ el 1 (x 1 , t) dt = σ c b πG 1 ∂ ∂t f V t + b -x 1 b (71)
div.p: As a result, Eq. ( 69) becomes
div.formula: δ 1 (x 1 , t) = π t -∞ C 1 (t -τ ) ∂ ∂τ f V τ + b -x 1 b dτ(72)
div.p: To obtain the crack tip stretch due to material 1 we set x 1 = V t so that
div.formula: δ 1 (V t, t) = σ c b π t t-b V C 1 (t -τ ) ∂ ∂τ f b -V (t -τ ) b dτ(73)
div.p: where the lower limit on the integration arises as that is the time material point that is the crack tip at time t entered the cohesive zone. We note that
div.formula: λ = b -V (t -τ ) b(74)
div.p: Thus, we can write
enabling a change of the integration variable so that Eq. ( 73) becomes
div.formula: δ tip 1 = σ c b π 1 0 C 1 b V (λ -1) df (λ) dλ dλ(76)
div.p: where, as above,
and thus df (λ)
The equivalent result for material 2 is
div.formula: δ tip 2 = σ c b π 1 0 C 2 b V (λ -1) df (λ) dλ dλ(79)
div.p: Note that for a Maxwell material
where µ is the viscosity. Consequently,
div.formula: 1 0 C 1 b V (λ -1) df (λ) dλ dλ = 1 G 1 + b 3µ 1 V (81)
div.p: It follows that the crack tip stretch for a Maxwell bimaterial crack where the materials are incompressible is
div.formula: δ tip = δ tip 1 + δ tip 2 = 1 G 1 + 1 G 2 + b 3V 1 µ 1 + 1 µ 2 σ c b π (82)

figure.head: Fig. 1 -
figure.figDesc: Fig.1 -The crack of size a >> b where b is a cohesive region, propagating at the interface between an elastic and a viscoelastic material

figure.head: Fig. 2 -
figure.figDesc: Fig.2-The dimensionless speed of subcritical crack propagation from equation (23) for n = 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1 (black, blue, red, green solid lines) in a viscoelastic/elastic bimaterial interface for a semi-infinite crack, as a function of dimensionless stress intensity factor

figure.head: Fig. 3 -
figure.figDesc: Fig.3-The maximum toughness amplification A max from (26) (black thick solid line) as a function of the ratio E/E 0 , where E is the elastic modulus of the elastic material, and E 0 the relaxed modulus of the viscoelastic one.Blue dashed line is the simplified form A max = E E 0

figure.head: Figure 4 -
figure.figDesc: Figure 4 -Stretch due to the cohesive zone tractions only from Eq.(57) for ε = 0, (black thick solid line), and for ε = 0.1, 0.2, 0.3 to second order with blue, red and green lines.

row.cell: cohesive strength and toughness for crack growth in visco-elastic
Wang, X.-M. , Shen, Y.-P. "The Dugdale crack on a bimaterial
interface," International Journal of Fracture, 59 pp. R25-R32.
Williams, M. L.; Landel, R. F.; Ferry, J. D. (1955) The Temperature
Dependence of Relaxation Mechanisms in Amorphous Polymers and Other
Glass-Forming Liquids. Journal of the American Chemical Society, 77, 3701-

note.p: Obviously we could add the elastic halfplane compliance in the C (t) and C ef f function, and then remove the elastic contribution to G, and the results would not change.

div.head: Acknowledgements
div.p: MC acknowledges support from the Italian Ministry of Education, University and Research (MIUR) under the program "Departments of Excellence" (L.232/2016). RM was supported by the MRSEC program of the U.S. National Science Program through Grant No. DMR-1720256 (IRG3).

analytic.title: Mechanics of elastomeric adhesion

monogr.title: Journal of Polymer Science: Polymer Symposia

analytic.title: Steady-state propagation of a crack in a viscoelastic strip
Tackiness of elastomers
Viscoelastic creep crack growth: a review of fracture mechanical analyses
Non-linear fracture mechanics
The growth of a dominant crack in a creeping material
The interface crack
The mechanics of adhesion of viscoelastic solids Phil
The theory of viscoelastic crack propagation and healing

monogr.title: Journal of Physics D: Applied Physics

analytic.title: Adhesion of viscoelastic materials to rigid substrates

monogr.title: Proceedings of the Royal Society of London. A. Mathematical and Physical Sciences

analytic.title: Two extending crack problems in linear viscoelasticity theory Q
Thin-film peeling-the elastic term

monogr.title: Journal of Physics D: Applied Physics

analytic.title: Fracture mechanics and adherence of viscoelastic solids
Crack propagation in viscoelastic solids
Plane problems of cracks in dissimilar media
Elastic fracture mechanics concepts for interfacial cracks

monogr.title: Viscoelastic crack propagation: review of theories and applications

analytic.title: A theory of crack initiation and growth in viscoelastic media
A theory of crack initiation and growth in viscoelastic media II. Approximate methods of analysis
Interface crack between two elastic layers
